# Node.js SDK Development Guide

How pi-a2a-communication works as a Node.js library and pi extension package, best practices for development, testing, CI/CD, and deployment. Covers the pi extension SDK, TypeScript library patterns, and real-world deployment scenarios.

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Architecture: How This Library Works](#architecture-how-this-library-works)
2. [Pi Extension System](#pi-extension-system)
3. [TypeScript Library Patterns](#typescript-library-patterns)
4. [Testing Best Practices](#testing-best-practices)
5. [Build and Publishing](#build-and-publishing)
6. [CI/CD Practices](#cicd-practices)
7. [Common Deployment Scenarios](#common-deployment-scenarios)
8. [References and Further Reading](#references-and-further-reading)

---

## Architecture: How This Library Works

### What It Is

pi-a2a-communication is a **TypeScript Node.js library** packaged as a **pi extension**. It has no frontend, no web framework, and no runtime dependencies beyond Node.js itself.

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Type** | Backend library / pi extension package (plugin) |
| **Language** | TypeScript 5.x, targeting ES2022 |
| **Module system** | NodeNext (ESM primary, CJS secondary via dual build) |
| **Runtime** | Node.js ≥ 18 |
| **Runtime deps** | Zero (all logic is self-contained) |
| **Dev deps** | TypeScript, Vitest, ESLint, `@types/node` |
| **UI** | None — headless extension registering slash commands and tools |
| **Testing** | Vitest with V8 coverage |

### Source Architecture

```
src/
├── index.ts           # Extension entry point — registers all commands/tools with pi
├── a2a-client.ts      # A2A client: sendMessage, sendStreamingMessage, getTask, cancelTask
├── a2a-server.ts      # A2A server: HTTP listener, JSON-RPC, SSE, task lifecycle
├── agent-discovery.ts # Agent Card fetching, parsing, caching, registry
├── task-manager.ts    # Task orchestration: single, parallel, chain, async
├── config.ts          # Configuration manager with disk persistence
├── types.ts           # A2A v1.0 protocol type definitions + constants
└── enterprise-config.example.ts  # Enterprise config example
```

The entry point (`index.ts`) exports a **default factory function** that receives the pi `ExtensionAPI`. This is the pi extension contract — the only thing pi needs to load your extension.

### How It Connects to pi

```typescript
// index.ts — the pi extension contract
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  // Subscribe to events
  pi.on("session_start", async (event, ctx) => { /* ... */ });

  // Register tools the LLM can call
  pi.registerTool({ name: "a2a_call", /* ... */ });
  pi.registerTool({ name: "a2a_parallel", /* ... */ });

  // Register slash commands
  pi.registerCommand("a2a-discover", { /* ... */ });
  // ... 10 commands total
}
```

Pi discovers this extension via:
- **Global path:** `~/.pi/agent/extensions/pi-a2a-communication/index.ts` (auto-discovered)
- **Project path:** `.pi/extensions/pi-a2a-communication/index.ts`
- **Package install:** `pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication`

When pi starts a session, it loads the extension's default export, calls it with `ExtensionAPI`, and the extension wires itself into the event system.

---

## Pi Extension System

### Core Concepts

Pi extensions are **TypeScript modules** that hook into the pi agent lifecycle. They are loaded via [jiti](https://github.com/unjs/jiti) — no compilation step required for development.

Key capabilities available to extensions:

| Capability | API | This Library's Usage |
|-----------|-----|---------------------|
| **Custom tools** | `pi.registerTool()` | `a2a_call`, `a2a_parallel` |
| **Slash commands** | `pi.registerCommand()` | `/a2a-discover`, `/a2a-send`, etc. (10 commands) |
| **Event hooks** | `pi.on(event, handler)` | `session_start`, `session_shutdown` |
| **User interaction** | `ctx.ui.notify()`, `ctx.ui.confirm()` | Status messages, error notifications |
| **Session state** | `ctx.sessionManager` | Task state persistence |

### Extension Lifecycle

```
pi starts
  └─► Loads extensions from auto-discovery paths
      └─► Calls export default function(pi: ExtensionAPI)
          └─► Extension registers tools, commands, event handlers
              └─► session_start fires → extension initializes runtime state
                  └─► Agent processes prompts → tools/commands invoked
                      └─► session_shutdown fires → extension cleans up resources
```

### Extension Styles

**Single file** (simplest):
```
~/.pi/agent/extensions/
└── my-extension.ts
```

**Directory with index.ts** (this project's pattern):
```
~/.pi/agent/extensions/
└── pi-a2a-communication/
    ├── index.ts        # Entry point (default export)
    ├── a2a-client.ts   # Helper module
    ├── a2a-server.ts   # Helper module
    └── ...              # Other modules
```

**Package with dependencies** (for npm-published packages):
```json
{
  "name": "pi-a2a-communication",
  "dependencies": { /* runtime deps */ },
  "pi": {
    "extensions": ["./dist/index.js"],
    "commands": ["/a2a-discover", "/a2a-send", "..."],
    "tools": ["a2a_call", "a2a_parallel"]
  }
}
```

### Available Imports

Extensions can import from these pi packages:

| Package | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` | `ExtensionAPI`, `ExtensionContext`, event types, session types |
| `typebox` | Schema definitions for tool parameters (TypeBox) |
| `@earendil-works/pi-ai` | AI utilities (`StringEnum` for Google-compatible enums) |
| `@earendil-works/pi-tui` | TUI components for custom rendering |

This project uses **type stubs** (`types/pi-runtime.d.ts`) that redirect the `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent` import (the old package name) to local type definitions. This allows development without the full pi runtime installed:

```jsonc
// tsconfig.json paths
{
  "paths": {
    "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent": ["./types/pi-runtime.d.ts"],
    "@mariozechner/pi-ai": ["./types/pi-runtime.d.ts"],
    "@mariozechner/pi-tui": ["./types/pi-runtime.d.ts"],
    "@mariozechner/pi-agent-core": ["./types/pi-runtime.d.ts"]
  }
}
```

### Important Rules for pi Extensions

1. **Don't start background resources in the factory function.** The factory may run in invocations that never start a session. Defer to `session_start`.

2. **Clean up in `session_shutdown`.** Register an idempotent shutdown handler to close servers, connections, file watchers.

3. **Use `ctx.signal` for abort-aware async work.** This lets users cancel operations with Esc.

4. **Return values from event handlers control behavior.** For `tool_call`, return `{ block: true, reason: "..." }` to block. For `input`, return `{ action: "handled" }` to skip agent processing.

5. **Extensions run with full system permissions.** Only install extensions from trusted sources.

### Pi Package Installation

```bash
# Install from git
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication

# Install from npm
pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@0.2.0

# Install from local path
pi install /path/to/pi-a2a-communication

# Try without installing (temporary)
pi -e git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication

# List installed packages
pi list

# Update all packages
pi update --all

# Remove
pi remove npm:pi-a2a-communication
```

Pi packages go to `~/.pi/agent/npm/` (npm) or `~/.pi/agent/git/` (git) for global scope, `.pi/npm/` or `.pi/git/` for project scope.

---

## TypeScript Library Patterns

### Dual Build: ESM + CJS

This project produces dual output formats:

```
dist/
├── index.js          # ESM (primary)
├── index.cjs          # CJS (secondary)
├── index.d.ts         # Type declarations
└── ...                # Other modules
```

Configured in `package.json`:
```json
{
  "type": "module",
  "main": "dist/index.js",
  "exports": {
    ".": {
      "import": "./dist/index.js",
      "require": "./dist/index.cjs",
      "types": "./dist/index.d.ts"
    }
  }
}
```

And built with two TypeScript configs:
- `tsconfig.json` → ESM output (`dist/index.js`)
- `tsconfig.cjs.json` → CJS output (`dist/index.cjs`)

```bash
# Build both
npm run build
# → tsc && npm run build:cjs
# → tsc -p tsconfig.cjs.json && mv dist/cjs/index.js dist/index.cjs
```

### Zero Runtime Dependencies

This project has **zero runtime dependencies** — all logic is self-contained. This is a deliberate choice for:

- **Minimal install footprint** — no transitive dependency conflicts
- **Deterministic builds** — no dependency version drift
- **Security** — smaller attack surface, no supply chain risk from transitive deps
- **pi compatibility** — no version conflicts with pi's own dependencies

Dev dependencies only:

```json
{
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/node": "^20.0.0",
    "@typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin": "^6.0.0",
    "@typescript-eslint/parser": "^6.0.0",
    "eslint": "^8.0.0",
    "typescript": "^5.0.0",
    "vitest": "^1.6.1"
  }
}
```

### Module Resolution: NodeNext

```jsonc
// tsconfig.json
{
  "target": "ES2022",
  "module": "NodeNext",
  "moduleResolution": "NodeNext"
}
```

This means:
- **Source imports must include `.js` extensions:** `import { A2AClient } from "./a2a-client.js"`
- **Node.js resolves ESM natively** — no bundler required
- **CJS consumers** get `dist/index.cjs` via the `require` export condition

### Type Stub Pattern for Host Decoupling

This project doesn't depend on `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent` at runtime. Instead, it uses **type stubs**:

```
types/
└── pi-runtime.d.ts    # Declares ExtensionAPI, ExtensionContext, etc.
```

With `tsconfig.json` paths redirecting the import:
```jsonc
{
  "paths": {
    "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent": ["./types/pi-runtime.d.ts"]
  }
}
```

And `vitest.config.ts` mirroring the alias:
```typescript
resolve: {
  alias: {
    "@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent": path.resolve(SRC, "types/pi-runtime.d.ts"),
  }
}
```

**Benefits:**
- Develop and test without the full pi runtime installed
- No version coupling between the extension and pi
- At runtime, pi provides the real `ExtensionAPI` — types are only for development
- Tests can mock the pi API with simple test doubles

### Global Extension State Pattern

The extension uses module-level state variables, initialized on `session_start`:

```typescript
let a2aClient: A2AClient | null = null;
let a2aServer: A2AServer | null = null;
let agentDiscovery: AgentDiscovery | null = null;
let taskManager: TaskManager | null = null;
let configManager: ConfigManager | null = null;

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("session_start", async (event, ctx) => {
    configManager = new ConfigManager(DEFAULT_CONFIG);
    a2aClient = new A2AClient(configManager.getConfig().client, ...);
    // ...
  });

  pi.on("session_shutdown", async () => {
    await a2aServer?.stop();
    a2aClient = null;
    // ...
  });
}
```

This pattern:
- **Defers initialization** to session start (avoids the anti-pattern of starting servers in the factory)
- **Cleans up** on session shutdown
- **Allows null checks** in tools/commands to handle pre-initialization state

---

## Testing Best Practices

### Test Architecture

This project uses a **three-tier test strategy** per anti-drift rule CA-5 ("All new features start with a test"):

| Tier | Directory | Purpose | When to Run |
|------|-----------|---------|-------------|
| **Conformance** | `tests/a2a-v1-conformance.test.ts` | A2A v1.0 spec compliance (19 tests, S1–S6b) | Every commit touching server/protocol code |
| **Spec Compliance** | `tests/spec-compliance/` | Unit-level spec compliance tests | Every commit touching `types.ts` |
| **Characterization** | `tests/characterization/` | Existing behavior documentation tests | Before refactoring |

### Vitest Configuration

```typescript
// vitest.config.ts
export default defineConfig({
  test: {
    globals: true,
    environment: 'node',
    include: ['tests/**/*.test.ts'],
    testTimeout: 15000,
    hookTimeout: 15000,
    coverage: {
      provider: 'v8',
      reporter: ['text', 'json', 'html'],
      include: ['src/**/*.ts'],
      exclude: ['tests/**', 'dist/**', 'types/**']
    }
  },
  resolve: {
    conditions: ['node'],
    alias: {
      // pi runtime type stubs (not real runtime)
      '@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent': path.resolve(SRC, 'types/pi-runtime.d.ts'),
      // ... other stubs
    }
  }
});
```

### Running Tests

```bash
# Run all tests
npx vitest run

# Run only conformance tests
npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance

# Run with coverage
npx vitest run --coverage

# Run in watch mode
npx vitest

# Run a single test file
npx vitest run tests/spec-compliance/a2a-v1-protocol.test.ts
```

### TDD Workflow (per CA-5)

1. **Write a failing test** first
   - Spec gap → write a conformance test (e.g., "S7: server must return JSON-RPC error for unknown method")
   - Existing behavior → write a characterization test
   - Edge case → write a unit test

2. **Implement** the minimum code to make it pass

3. **Verify** by running the relevant test suite:
   ```bash
   npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance  # After any protocol change
   npx vitest run spec-compliance      # After any types.ts change
   ```

4. **Refactor** if needed, keeping all tests green

### Mocking the pi Extension API

Since the extension uses type stubs, tests need to mock `ExtensionAPI` and `ExtensionContext`:

```typescript
// Create a mock ExtensionAPI
const mockPi = {
  on: vi.fn(),
  registerTool: vi.fn(),
  registerCommand: vi.fn(),
  registerShortcut: vi.fn(),
  registerFlag: vi.fn(),
  sendMessage: vi.fn(),
  appendEntry: vi.fn(),
};

// Create a mock ExtensionContext
const mockCtx = {
  ui: {
    notify: vi.fn(),
    confirm: vi.fn(),
    select: vi.fn(),
    input: vi.fn(),
  },
  cwd: '/tmp/test',
  sessionManager: { /* ... */ },
};

// Call the extension factory
import extension from '../src/index.js';
extension(mockPi as any);
```

For HTTP-based testing (A2A client/server), use Vitest's built-in fetch mocking or start a real HTTP server on a random port:

```typescript
import { Server } from 'node:http';

// Start server on random port
const server = new Server(app);
await new Promise(resolve => server.listen(0, resolve));
const port = server.address().port;
const baseUrl = `http://localhost:${port}`;

// ... run tests against baseUrl ...

// Cleanup
server.close();
```

---

## Build and Publishing

### Build Process

```bash
# Clean and build both ESM + CJS
npm run build
# → tsc && npm run build:cjs
# → tsc -p tsconfig.cjs.json && mv dist/cjs/index.js dist/index.cjs
```

Output structure:
```
dist/
├── index.js           # ESM entry point
├── index.cjs          # CJS entry point
├── index.d.ts         # TypeScript declarations
├── index.d.ts.map     # Declaration source maps
├── a2a-client.js      # Individual modules (ESM)
├── a2a-server.js
├── ...
└── a2a-client.d.ts    # Individual declarations
```

### Pi Package Manifest

The `pi-package.json` is the pi-specific manifest (separate from `package.json`):

```json
{
  "name": "pi-a2a-communication",
  "version": "0.1.0-alpha.1",
  "description": "A2A client extension for pi",
  "author": "Carlos Frias <carlos@frias.io>",
  "category": "communication",
  "tags": ["a2a", "multi-agent", "distributed", "communication", "pi-extension"],
  "repository": "https://github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication",
  "compatibility": {
    "pi": ">=1.0.0",
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "commands": ["/a2a-discover", "/a2a-send", "..."],
  "tools": ["a2a_call", "a2a_parallel"]
}
```

### Publishing to npm

```bash
# Ensure clean build
npm run build

# Dry run
npm publish --dry-run

# Publish (after version bump)
npm version patch  # or minor, major
npm publish
```

### Publishing as a Pi Package (git)

```bash
# Tag a release
git tag v0.2.0
git push origin v0.2.0

# Users install with
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0
```

Pi handles `npm install` automatically during `pi install`. Runtime dependencies in `package.json` `dependencies` are installed; `devDependencies` are skipped.

---

## CI/CD Practices

### Recommended GitHub Actions Workflow

This project does not yet have a `.github/` directory. Here is a recommended CI/CD setup:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
          cache: npm
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - run: npx vitest run
      - run: npx vitest run --coverage

  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint

  publish:
    needs: [test, lint]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && startsWith(github.event.head_commit.message, 'release')
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - run: npm publish
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

### Key CI/CD Practices for Node.js Libraries

1. **Matrix testing across Node versions** — Test on 18, 20, 22 minimum
2. **Build verification** — `npm run build` must succeed before tests
3. **Conformance tests as gate** — `npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance` must pass for any merge
4. **Coverage tracking** — V8 coverage with HTML report
5. **Lint before merge** — ESLint must pass
6. **Semantic versioning** — `npm version patch|minor|major` before publish
7. **Trusted publishing** — Use OIDC-based npm publishing from GitHub Actions (no long-lived tokens)
8. **Dual build verification** — Both ESM and CJS outputs must build cleanly

### Trusted Publishing (npm)

npm supports **trusted publishers** using OIDC authentication from GitHub Actions:

```yaml
# In publish job
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: 20
    registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org
- run: npm publish --provenance
  env:
    NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

This eliminates the need for long-lived npm tokens. Configure at [npmjs.com](https://www.npmjs.com) → Package → Settings → Trusted Publishers.

### Pre-publish Checklist

```bash
# 1. Update version
npm version patch  # or minor/major

# 2. Verify build
npm run build

# 3. Run all tests
npx vitest run

# 4. Run conformance suite specifically
npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance

# 5. Dry run publish
npm publish --dry-run

# 6. Publish
npm publish

# 7. Tag and push
git push origin main --tags
```

---

## Common Deployment Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Local Development Installation

The most common deployment — install the extension on your local pi agent for development and testing.

```bash
# Install from git (recommended for development)
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication

# Or link locally for active development
cd ~/projects/pi-a2a-communication
npm link
# Then in pi settings.json, add to extensions:
# "/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts"
```

**Use case:** Individual developer using A2A to communicate with agents running on other machines or services.

### Scenario 2: Fleet Deployment (Multi-Node)

Deploy the extension across a fleet of pi agents running on different machines. The A2A server mode exposes each pi agent as an A2A-compliant agent.

```bash
# On each fleet node:
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication

# Configure the server
/a2a-config server.enabled true
/a2a-config server.port 10000
/a2a-config server.host 0.0.0.0

# Start the A2A server
/a2a-server start 10000
```

**Use case:** A fleet of specialized pi agents (research, code review, deployment) communicating via A2A protocol across machines.

### Scenario 3: A2A Gateway (Enterprise)

For production deployments, the A2A server code in this package is a stub. The real production deployment uses **pi-a2a-gateway** (a separate project) that provides:

- TLS termination
- Authentication/authorization (OAuth2, mTLS)
- Rate limiting
- Agent registry
- Health monitoring

This package is the **client** that connects to the gateway.

```
┌─────────────────┐      A2A Protocol      ┌──────────────────┐
│  pi Agent       │ ◄──────────────────► │  A2A Gateway     │
│  (this package) │    JSON-RPC over HTTP  │  (pi-a2a-gateway)│
└─────────────────┘                        └────────┬─────────┘
                                                    │
                                    ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
                                    │               │               │
                                    ▼               ▼               ▼
                            ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
                            │ Auth Agent   │ │ Billing Agent│ │ ML Agent     │
                            └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘
```

**Use case:** Enterprise multi-organization agent collaboration with security, rate limiting, and centralized management.

### Scenario 4: CI/CD Pipeline Integration

Use `a2a_call` and `a2a_parallel` tools in pi sessions to orchestrate CI/CD tasks across multiple agents.

```bash
# In a pi session triggered by CI/CD:
# 1. Discover available agents
/a2a-discover https://ci-agent-1.example.com
/a2a-discover https://ci-agent-2.example.com

# 2. Run security scans in parallel
/a2a-broadcast "Scan for vulnerabilities" \
  --agents https://security-agent.ci.com,https://compliance-agent.ci.com

# 3. Generate documentation
/a2a-send docs-agent "Generate release notes for v2.0"
```

**Use case:** Automated CI/CD pipelines where pi orchestrates multiple specialized agents for code review, security scanning, and documentation generation.

### Scenario 5: SDK Embedding

Use the pi SDK to embed A2A capabilities in a custom application:

```typescript
import { createAgentSession, DefaultResourceLoader, SessionManager } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

const loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({
  additionalExtensionPaths: ["/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/dist/index.js"],
});
await loader.reload();

const { session } = await createAgentSession({
  resourceLoader: loader,
  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
});

// Now the session has a2a_call and a2a_parallel tools available
await session.prompt("Use a2a_call to check the security agent at https://security.example.com");
```

**Use case:** Building a custom web UI, CLI, or automated pipeline that uses pi as an agent with A2A capabilities.

### Scenario 6: Development Testing with Local Server

For development, start the local A2A server and test client calls against it:

```bash
# Terminal 1: Start the A2A server
/a2a-server start 10000

# Terminal 2: Discover and call
/a2a-discover http://localhost:10000
/a2a-send localhost-agent "Test message"
```

**Use case:** Local development and testing without needing a remote A2A gateway.

### Integrating with Other pi Extensions

pi-a2a-communication can coexist and collaborate with other pi extensions. Common integration patterns:

#### Pattern 1: Extension Event Coordination

Extensions communicate through pi's event system. A2A can be triggered by other extensions' events:

```typescript
// In another extension (e.g., pi-playwright-maintenance)
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  pi.on("tool_call", async (event, ctx) => {
    // When a browser test fails, delegate analysis to an A2A agent
    if (event.toolName === "playwright" && event.input.includes("--test")) {
      pi.sendUserMessage("/a2a-send qa-agent "Analyze this test failure"", {
        deliverAs: "followUp"
      });
    }
  });
}
```

#### Pattern 2: Tool Chaining

A2A tools (`a2a_call`, `a2a_parallel`) can be used alongside built-in pi tools in agent prompts:

```
User: Research the latest A2A spec changes and then review our conformance

Agent: I'll use a2a_call to research, then review locally.
→ a2a_call(research-agent, "Find latest A2A v1.0 spec changes")
→ read(tests/a2a-v1-conformance.test.ts)
→ edit(tests/...)  // Apply conformance fixes
```

#### Pattern 3: Coms-Net Bridge (Legacy)

The project previously used coms-net for inter-node communication. A2A replaces this:

| Coms-Net Feature | A2A Equivalent |
|------------------|---------------|
| `coms_net_send` | `a2a_call` (tool) or `/a2a-send` (command) |
| `coms_net_list` | `/a2a-discover` + `/a2a-agents` |
| Broadcast | `/a2a-broadcast` or `a2a_parallel` |
| Hub coordination | A2A Gateway (pi-a2a-gateway) |

### Integrating with External Systems

#### REST API Integration

The A2A client uses standard HTTP/JSON-RPC. Any REST-capable system can be wrapped as an A2A agent:

```typescript
// External system exposes an Agent Card + JSON-RPC endpoint
// a2a-client.ts handles the protocol layer
const client = new A2AClient(clientConfig, securityConfig);
const result = await client.sendMessage({
  agentUrl: "https://api.example.com/a2a",
  message: { role: "user", parts: [{ type: "text", text: "Query" }] },
  contextId: "session-123",
});
```

#### MCP (Model Context Protocol) Coexistence

A2A and MCP serve complementary roles:

| | A2A | MCP |
|--|-----|-----|
| **Purpose** | Agent ↔ Agent communication | Agent ↔ Tool integration |
| **Analogy** | Colleagues delegating work | Worker using a tool |
| **This project** | ✅ What we implement | ❌ Not our scope |
| **Together** | pi calls an A2A agent → that agent uses MCP tools internally ||

A pi agent can use both simultaneously:
- **MCP** for accessing local tools (databases, file systems, APIs)
- **A2A** for delegating tasks to remote agents

#### Message Queue Integration

For async or high-throughput scenarios, A2A can work alongside message queues:

```
┌────────────┐    A2A/JSON-RPC     ┌────────────┐
│  pi Agent  │ ◄────────────────► │ A2A Server │
└─────┬──────┘                    └──────┬─────┘
      │                                  │
      │ Events/Commands                  │ Task Results
      ▼                                  ▼
┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐
│  Message   │                    │   Redis    │
│   Queue    │                    │   Cache    │
│ (Redis/GCP)│                    │  (Tasks)   │
└────────────┘                    └────────────┘
```

The `TaskManager` in `task-manager.ts` supports async task patterns:
- **Fire-and-forget:** Send a task, get a task ID, poll later
- **Streaming:** Get SSE updates as the task progresses
- **Push notifications:** A2A server sends callbacks when tasks complete

#### Webhook Integration

A2A push notifications enable webhook-style integration:

```typescript
// Configure push notification callback
const result = await client.sendMessage({
  agentUrl: "https://agent.example.com/a2a",
  message: { role: "user", parts: [{ type: "text", text: "Long-running analysis" }] },
  pushNotification: {
    url: "https://your-server.example.com/webhook/a2a",
    authentication: { scheme: "bearer", credential: "token" }
  }
});
```

### Integration Testing Patterns

#### Testing A2A Client Against a Real Server

```typescript
// tests/integration/a2a-client-integration.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect, beforeAll, afterAll } from "vitest";
import { A2AServer } from "../../src/a2a-server.js";
import { A2AClient } from "../../src/a2a-client.js";

describe("A2A Client-Server Integration", () => {
  let server: A2AServer;
  let baseUrl: string;

  beforeAll(async () => {
    server = new A2AServer({ port: 0, host: "127.0.0.1" });
    await server.start();
    baseUrl = `http://127.0.0.1:${server.port}`;
  });

  afterAll(async () => {
    await server.stop();
  });

  it("should discover agent card", async () => {
    const discovery = new AgentDiscovery({ cacheEnabled: false });
    const card = await discovery.discoverAgent(baseUrl);
    expect(card).toBeDefined();
    expect(card.name).toBe("pi-a2a-agent");
  });

  it("should send message and receive response", async () => {
    const client = new A2AClient(clientConfig, securityConfig);
    const result = await client.sendMessage({
      agentUrl: baseUrl,
      message: { role: "user", parts: [{ type: "text", text: "Hello" }] },
    });
    expect(result.status).toBe("completed");
  });
});
```

#### Testing Extension Registration

```typescript
// tests/unit/extension-registration.test.ts
import { describe, it, expect, vi } from "vitest";
import extension from "../../src/index.js";

describe("Extension Registration", () => {
  it("should register all 10 commands", () => {
    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };
    extension(mockPi as any);
    expect(mockPi.registerCommand).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(10);
  });

  it("should register 2 tools", () => {
    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };
    extension(mockPi as any);
    expect(mockPi.registerTool).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(2);
  });

  it("should subscribe to session_start and session_shutdown", () => {
    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };
    extension(mockPi as any);
    expect(mockPi.on).toHaveBeenCalledWith("session_start", expect.any(Function));
    expect(mockPi.on).toHaveBeenCalledWith("session_shutdown", expect.any(Function));
  });
});
```

---

## Release & Distribution Practices

### Versioning Strategy

This project follows **Semantic Versioning (semver)** with a pre-release phase:

```
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH[-PRERELEASE]

Examples:
0.1.0-alpha.1   ← Pre-alpha: incomplete features, breaking changes expected

0.2.0-alpha.1   ← Alpha: feature-complete for milestone, spec compliance in progress

0.2.0-beta.1     ← Beta: all features working, testing for edge cases

0.2.0            ← Stable: ready for production use

1.0.0            ← Major: A2A v1.0 spec fully compliant, API stable
```

**When to bump each level:**

| Change | Bump | Example |
|--------|------|--------|
| A2A protocol method changed (breaking) | MAJOR | `1.0.0` → `2.0.0` |
| New command/tool added (non-breaking) | MINOR | `0.2.0` → `0.3.0` |
| Spec compliance fix, bug fix | PATCH | `0.2.1` → `0.2.2` |
| Spec compliance work in progress | PRERELEASE | `0.2.0-alpha.1` → `0.2.0-alpha.2` |

### Dual Manifest Management

This project maintains **two manifests** that must be kept in sync:

| File | Purpose | Key Fields |
|------|---------|------------|
| `package.json` | npm package (build, dependencies, scripts) | `name`, `version`, `main`, `exports`, `scripts`, `devDependencies` |
| `pi-package.json` | pi extension (commands, tools, compatibility) | `name`, `version`, `commands`, `tools`, `compatibility`, `category` |

**Synchronization checklist on version bump:**

1. Update `version` in both `package.json` and `pi-package.json`
2. Verify `commands` and `tools` in `pi-package.json` match `index.ts` registrations
3. Verify `compatibility.node` matches `engines.node` in `package.json`
4. Update `changes` array in `pi-package.json` with release notes
5. Run `npm run build` to verify both manifests produce valid output

### Release Workflow

#### For Pre-release (alpha/beta)

```bash
# 1. Ensure all tests pass
npx vitest run
npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance

# 2. Bump pre-release version
npm version prerelease --preid=alpha  # 0.2.0-alpha.1 → 0.2.0-alpha.2
# OR manually:
npm version 0.2.0-alpha.2

# 3. Update pi-package.json version to match
# (manual edit or script)

# 4. Build
npm run build

# 5. Publish to npm with pre-release tag
npm publish --tag alpha

# 6. Git tag and push
git tag v0.2.0-alpha.2
git push origin main --tags

# 7. Users install with
cd ~ && pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@alpha
```

#### For Stable Release

```bash
# 1. Ensure all tests pass (including conformance)
npx vitest run
npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance

# 2. Update CHANGELOG (or let automation handle it)
# See "Changelog" section below

# 3. Bump version
npm version patch  # 0.2.0 → 0.2.1 (bug fix)
npm version minor  # 0.2.0 → 0.3.0 (new feature)
npm version major  # 0.x.0 → 1.0.0 (breaking change)

# 4. Update pi-package.json version to match

# 5. Build and verify
npm run build
npm publish --dry-run

# 6. Publish to npm
npm publish

# 7. Git tag and push
git tag v0.2.1
git push origin main --tags

# 8. Users install with
pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@0.2.1
# OR
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.1
```

### Distribution Channels

This project supports **three distribution channels**:

| Channel | Install Command | Use Case |
|---------|----------------|----------|
| **npm** | `pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@0.2.0` | Production, version-pinned installs |
| **Git** | `pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0` | Development, custom branches, unreleased features |
| **Local** | `pi install ./path/to/pi-a2a-communication` | Local development, testing unreleased changes |

#### npm Distribution

```jsonc
// package.json — fields that control npm distribution
{
  "name": "pi-a2a-communication",
  "version": "0.2.0-alpha.1",
  "main": "dist/index.js",
  "types": "dist/index.d.ts",
  "files": [
    "dist/**/*",
    "README.md",
    "LICENSE"
  ],
  "keywords": ["pi", "pi-extension", "pi-package", "a2a", "agent2agent", ...],
  "repository": "https://github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication"
}
```

**Key points:**
- `files` field controls what's included in the npm tarball — only `dist/`, `README.md`, and `LICENSE`
- Source maps and declarations are included for debugging
- `keywords` must include `pi-package` for gallery discoverability

#### Git Distribution

Pi handles git packages by:
1. Cloning the repo to `~/.pi/agent/git/github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication/`
2. Checking out the pinned ref (tag, branch, or commit)
3. Running `npm install` (production only) if `package.json` exists
4. Loading the extension from the `pi.extensions` or `pi.manifest` field

**Best practices for git distribution:**
- Always pin to a version tag: `pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0`
- Never rely on `main` branch alone — branches can have breaking changes
- Ensure `npm install --omit=dev` succeeds in CI — all runtime deps must be in `dependencies`

#### Local Development Distribution

```bash
# Link for active development (changes reflect immediately)
cd ~/projects/pi-a2a-communication
npm link

# Or add to settings.json manually
# ~/.pi/agent/settings.json
{
  "extensions": [
    "/absolute/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts"
  ]
}

# Or use -e for quick testing
pi -e /path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts
```

**Note:** When using `npm link`, changes to `.ts` files require pi `/reload` to take effect. pi loads extensions via jiti, which compiles on-the-fly but caches.

### Changelog Management

Maintain a `CHANGELOG.md` following [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/) format:

```markdown
# Changelog

## [0.2.0-alpha.2] - 2026-06-20

### Added
- SendStreamingMessage SSE support (S4 compliance)
- Push notification client support (S5 compliance)
- Task cancellation via `tasks/cancel` (S3b compliance)

### Fixed
- Agent Card path corrected to `/.well-known/agent-card` (S1)
- JSON-RPC method names aligned with v1.0 spec (S2)

### Changed
- `a2a-server.ts` is now the primary target for M6 spec fixes
- `executePiTask()` documented as stub (CA-3)

## [0.1.0-alpha.1] - 2026-06-15

### Added
- Initial fork from DrOlu/pi-a2a-communication v1.0.1
- A2A client, server, discovery, task manager
- 10 slash commands, 2 tools
- Vitest test suite with conformance tests
```

### Automated Release (GitHub Actions)

Recommended release automation:

```yaml
# .github/workflows/release.yml
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - "v*"

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: npm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
      - run: npx vitest run
      - run: npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance

      # Publish to npm
      - run: npm publish --provenance
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}

      # Create GitHub release
      - uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
        with:
          generate_release_notes: true
```

This triggers automatically when you push a version tag:

```bash
npm version minor  # Creates commit + tag v0.3.0
git push origin main --tags  # Triggers release workflow
```

### Gallery Visibility

To appear in the [pi package gallery](https://pi.dev/packages), ensure:

1. `package.json` includes `"keywords": ["pi-package", ...]`
2. `pi-package.json` includes `category`, `tags`, and `compatibility`
3. The package is published to npm with public visibility
4. Optional: Add `video` or `image` fields to `pi-package.json` for gallery preview:

```jsonc
// pi-package.json
{
  "video": "https://example.com/demo.mp4",  // Autoplays on hover
  "image": "https://example.com/screenshot.png"  // Static preview
}
```

---

## Common Deployment Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Individual Developer (Local Agent)

The simplest deployment — one developer, one machine, calling remote A2A agents.

```bash
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication
```

**What happens:**
1. Pi clones the repo to `~/.pi/agent/git/github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication/`
2. Runs `npm install --omit=dev`
3. Loads `src/index.ts` via jiti (no build step needed for development)
4. Extension registers 10 commands and 2 tools
5. On session start, A2A client and discovery modules initialize

**Use case:** Individual developer using `/a2a-send` to delegate tasks to remote agents (e.g., a research agent, a code review agent).

### Scenario 2: Fleet Deployment (Multi-Node)

Deploy across multiple machines running pi agents, each exposing an A2A server.

```bash
# On each fleet node:
pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0

# Configure (persists to ~/.pi/agent/a2a/config.json)
/a2a-config server.enabled true
/a2a-config server.port 10000
/a2a-config server.host 0.0.0.0
/a2a-config security.defaultScheme bearer
/a2a-config security.bearerToken "$A2A_BEARER_TOKEN"

# Start the A2A server
/a2a-server start 10000
```

**Use case:** A fleet of 7 specialized pi agents (research, code review, deployment, monitoring, etc.) communicating via A2A protocol. Each agent discovers others via Agent Cards.

### Scenario 3: A2A Gateway (Enterprise)

For production deployments with TLS, auth, and rate limiting, use **pi-a2a-gateway** (a separate project). This package is the **client** that connects to the gateway.

```
┌─────────────────┐      A2A Protocol      ┌──────────────────┐
│  pi Agent       │ ◄──────────────────► │  A2A Gateway     │
│  (this package) │    JSON-RPC over HTTP  │  (pi-a2a-gateway)│
└─────────────────┘                        └────────┬─────────┘
                                                    │
                                    ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐
                                    │               │               │
                                    ▼               ▼               ▼
                            ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
                            │ Auth Agent   │ │ Billing Agent│ │ ML Agent     │
                            └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘
```

**Gateway provides:** TLS termination, OAuth2/mTLS authentication, rate limiting, agent registry, health monitoring, task queuing.

**Use case:** Enterprise multi-organization agent collaboration with centralized management and security.

### Scenario 4: CI/CD Pipeline Integration

Embed A2A tools in automated CI/CD pipelines via the pi SDK:

```typescript
import {
  createAgentSession,
  DefaultResourceLoader,
  SessionManager,
} from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

const loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({
  additionalExtensionPaths: ["/opt/pi-extensions/pi-a2a-communication"],
});
await loader.reload();

const { session } = await createAgentSession({
  resourceLoader: loader,
  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
});

// CI/CD pipeline: security scan → code review → deploy
await session.prompt(`
  Use a2a_parallel to run security scans on these agents:
  - https://security-agent.ci.internal
  - https://compliance-agent.ci.internal
  Then use a2a_call to request code review from https://review-agent.ci.internal
`);
```

**Use case:** Automated pipelines where pi orchestrates multiple specialized agents for code review, security scanning, compliance checking, and deployment.

### Scenario 5: A2A + MCP Combined Architecture

An agent uses **MCP for local tools** and **A2A for remote agents**:

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                pi Agent                        │
│                                                │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐  │
│  │  MCP Tools   │    │  A2A Extension        │  │
│  │  (local)     │    │  (remote agents)     │  │
│  │              │    │                      │  │
│  │ - Database   │    │ - Research Agent     │  │
│  │ - File System│    │ - Code Review Agent  │  │
│  │ - Git        │    │ - Deployment Agent   │  │
│  │ - Browser    │    │ - Analytics Agent    │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Use case:** A pi agent that uses MCP to access databases and file systems locally, while delegating complex tasks (research, code review, analysis) to remote A2A agents. The agent combines both to produce comprehensive results.

### Scenario 6: Multi-Tenant SaaS

An A2A gateway serving multiple organizations, each with their own agents:

```
┌──────────────────────────┐
│    Organization A        │    ┌───────────────────┐
│  ┌────────────────────┐  │    │  A2A Gateway      │
│  │  pi Agent A1       │──┼───►│  (pi-a2a-gateway)  │
│  │  (research)        │  │    │  - Auth (OAuth2)   │
│  └────────────────────┘  │    │  - Rate Limiting   │
│  ┌────────────────────┐  │    │  - Agent Registry  │
│  │  pi Agent A2       │──┼───►│  - Health Monitor  │
│  │  (code review)     │  │    └───────────────────┘
│  └────────────────────┘  │            │
└──────────────────────────┘            │
                                        │
┌──────────────────────────┐            │
│    Organization B        │            │
│  ┌────────────────────┐  │            │
│  │  pi Agent B1       │──┼────────────┘
│  │  (deployment)      │  │
│  └────────────────────┘  │
│  ┌────────────────────┐  │
│  │  pi Agent B2       │  │
│  │  (analytics)       │  │
│  └────────────────────┘  │
└──────────────────────────┘
```

**Configuration per tenant:**

```jsonc
// ~/.pi/agent/a2a/config.json (Organization A)
{
  "client": {
    "timeout": 30000,
    "retryAttempts": 3,
    "maxConcurrentTasks": 10
  },
  "security": {
    "defaultScheme": "oauth2",
    "oauth2Config": {
      "tokenUrl": "https://auth.org-a.example.com/oauth2/token",
      "clientId": "org-a-pi-agent",
      "clientSecret": "$ORG_A_CLIENT_SECRET"
    }
  }
}
```

**Use case:** SaaS platform where each organization has its own pi agents communicating through a shared A2A gateway with tenant isolation, authentication, and rate limiting.

### Scenario 7: SDK Embedding (Custom Application)

Embed pi with A2A capabilities in a custom application:

```typescript
import {
  createAgentSession,
  DefaultResourceLoader,
  SessionManager,
} from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

const loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({
  additionalExtensionPaths: ["/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/dist/index.js"],
});
await loader.reload();

const { session } = await createAgentSession({
  resourceLoader: loader,
  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
});

// Programmatic A2A usage
await session.prompt("Use a2a_call to check the security agent");
```

**Use case:** Building a custom web UI, CLI, or automated pipeline that uses pi as an agent with A2A capabilities embedded directly.

### Scenario 8: Development Testing with Local Server

For local development and testing:

```bash
# Terminal 1: Start the A2A server
/a2a-server start 10000

# Terminal 2: Discover and call
/a2a-discover http://localhost:10000
/a2a-send localhost-agent "Test message"

# Terminal 3: Run conformance tests
npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance
```

**Use case:** Local development and testing without needing a remote A2A gateway."}, {"oldText": "## References and Further Reading

[References and Further Reading](#references-and-further-reading)"}, {"oldText": "## Common Deployment Scenarios\n\n### Scenario 1: Local Development Installation\n\nThe most common deployment — install the extension on your local pi agent for development and testing.\n\n```bash\n# Install from git (recommended for development)\npi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication\n\n# Or link locally for active development\ncd ~/projects/pi-a2a-communication\nnpm link\n# Then in pi settings.json, add to extensions:\n# \"/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts\"\n```\n\n**Use case:** Individual developer using A2A to communicate with agents running on other machines or services.\n\n### Scenario 2: Fleet Deployment (Multi-Node)\n\nDeploy the extension across a fleet of pi agents running on different machines. The A2A server mode exposes each pi agent as an A2A-compliant agent.\n\n```bash\n# On each fleet node:\npi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication\n\n# Configure the server\n/a2a-config server.enabled true\n/a2a-config server.port 10000\n/a2a-config server.host 0.0.0.0\n\n# Start the A2A server\n/a2a-server start 10000\n```\n\n**Use case:** A fleet of specialized pi agents (research, code review, deployment) communicating via A2A protocol across machines.\n\n### Scenario 3: A2A Gateway (Enterprise)\n\nFor production deployments, the A2A server code in this package is a stub. The real production deployment uses **pi-a2a-gateway** (a separate project) that provides:\n\n- TLS termination\n- Authentication/authorization (OAuth2, mTLS)\n- Rate limiting\n- Agent registry\n- Health monitoring\n\nThis package is the **client** that connects to the gateway.\n\n```\n┌─────────────────┐      A2A Protocol      ┌──────────────────┐\n│  pi Agent       │ ◄──────────────────► │  A2A Gateway     │\n│  (this package) │    JSON-RPC over HTTP  │  (pi-a2a-gateway)│\n└─────────────────┘                        └────────┬─────────┘\n                                                    │\n                                    ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐\n                                    │               │               │\n                                    ▼               ▼               ▼\n                            ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐\n                            │ Auth Agent   │ │ Billing Agent│ │ ML Agent     │\n                            └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘\n```\n\n**Use case:** Enterprise multi-organization agent collaboration with security, rate limiting, and centralized management.\n\n### Scenario 4: CI/CD Pipeline Integration\n\nUse `a2a_call` and `a2a_parallel` tools in pi sessions to orchestrate CI/CD tasks across multiple agents.\n\n```bash\n# In a pi session triggered by CI/CD:\n# 1. Discover available agents\n/a2a-discover https://ci-agent-1.example.com\n/a2a-discover https://ci-agent-2.example.com\n\n# 2. Run security scans in parallel\n/a2a-broadcast \"Scan for vulnerabilities\" \\\n  --agents https://security-agent.ci.com,https://compliance-agent.ci.com\n\n# 3. Generate documentation\n/a2a-send docs-agent \"Generate release notes for v2.0\"\n```\n\n**Use case:** Automated CI/CD pipelines where pi orchestrates multiple specialized agents for code review, security scanning, and documentation generation.\n\n### Scenario 5: SDK Embedding\n\nUse the pi SDK to embed A2A capabilities in a custom application:\n\n```typescript\nimport { createAgentSession, DefaultResourceLoader, SessionManager } from \"@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent\";\n\nconst loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({\n  additionalExtensionPaths: [\"/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/dist/index.js\"],\n});\nawait loader.reload();\n\nconst { session } = await createAgentSession({\n  resourceLoader: loader,\n  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),\n});\n\n// Now the session has a2a_call and a2a_parallel tools available\nawait session.prompt(\"Use a2a_call to check the security agent at https://security.example.com\");\n```\n\n**Use case:** Building a custom web UI, CLI, or automated pipeline that uses pi as an agent with A2A capabilities.\n\n### Scenario 6: Development Testing with Local Server\n\nFor development, start the local A2A server and test client calls against it:\n\n```bash\n# Terminal 1: Start the A2A server\n/a2a-server start 10000\n\n# Terminal 2: Discover and call\n/a2a-discover http://localhost:10000\n/a2a-send localhost-agent \"Test message\"\n```\n\n**Use case:** Local development and testing without needing a remote A2A gateway.", "newText": "## Integration Practices\n\n### Integrating with Other pi Extensions\n\npi-a2a-communication can coexist and collaborate with other pi extensions. Common integration patterns:\n\n#### Pattern 1: Extension Event Coordination\n\nExtensions communicate through pi's event system. A2A can be triggered by other extensions' events:\n\n```typescript\n// In another extension (e.g., pi-playwright-maintenance)\nimport type { ExtensionAPI } from \"@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent\";\n\nexport default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {\n  pi.on(\"tool_call\", async (event, ctx) => {\n    // When a browser test fails, delegate analysis to an A2A agent\n    if (event.toolName === \"playwright\" && event.input.includes(\"--test\")) {\n      pi.sendUserMessage(\"/a2a-send qa-agent \"Analyze this test failure\"", {\n        deliverAs: \"followUp\"\n      });\n    }\n  });\n}\n```\n\n#### Pattern 2: Tool Chaining\n\nA2A tools (`a2a_call`, `a2a_parallel`) can be used alongside built-in pi tools in agent prompts:\n\n```\nUser: Research the latest A2A spec changes and then review our conformance\n\nAgent: I'll use a2a_call to research, then review locally.\n→ a2a_call(research-agent, \"Find latest A2A v1.0 spec changes\")\n→ read(tests/a2a-v1-conformance.test.ts)\n→ edit(tests/...)  // Apply conformance fixes\n```\n\n#### Pattern 3: Coms-Net Bridge (Legacy)\n\nThe project previously used coms-net for inter-node communication. A2A replaces this:\n\n| Coms-Net Feature | A2A Equivalent |\n|------------------|---------------|\n| `coms_net_send` | `a2a_call` (tool) or `/a2a-send` (command) |\n| `coms_net_list` | `/a2a-discover` + `/a2a-agents` |\n| Broadcast | `/a2a-broadcast` or `a2a_parallel` |\n| Hub coordination | A2A Gateway (pi-a2a-gateway) |\n\n### Integrating with External Systems\n\n#### REST API Integration\n\nThe A2A client uses standard HTTP/JSON-RPC. Any REST-capable system can be wrapped as an A2A agent:\n\n```typescript\n// External system exposes an Agent Card + JSON-RPC endpoint\n// a2a-client.ts handles the protocol layer\nconst client = new A2AClient(clientConfig, securityConfig);\nconst result = await client.sendMessage({\n  agentUrl: \"https://api.example.com/a2a\",\n  message: { role: \"user\", parts: [{ type: \"text\", text: \"Query\" }] },\n  contextId: \"session-123\",\n});\n```\n\n#### MCP (Model Context Protocol) Coexistence\n\nA2A and MCP serve complementary roles:\n\n| | A2A | MCP |\n|--|-----|-----|\n| **Purpose** | Agent ↔ Agent communication | Agent ↔ Tool integration |\n| **Analogy** | Colleagues delegating work | Worker using a tool |\n| **This project** | ✅ What we implement | ❌ Not our scope |\n| **Together** | pi calls an A2A agent → that agent uses MCP tools internally ||\n\nA pi agent can use both simultaneously:\n- **MCP** for accessing local tools (databases, file systems, APIs)\n- **A2A** for delegating tasks to remote agents\n\n#### Message Queue Integration\n\nFor async or high-throughput scenarios, A2A can work alongside message queues:\n\n```\n┌────────────┐    A2A/JSON-RPC     ┌────────────┐\n│  pi Agent  │ ◄────────────────► │ A2A Server │\n└─────┬──────┘                    └──────┬─────┘\n      │                                  │\n      │ Events/Commands                  │ Task Results\n      ▼                                  ▼\n┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐\n│  Message   │                    │   Redis    │\n│   Queue    │                    │   Cache    │\n│ (Redis/GCP)│                    │  (Tasks)   │\n└────────────┘                    └────────────┘\n```\n\nThe `TaskManager` in `task-manager.ts` supports async task patterns:\n- **Fire-and-forget:** Send a task, get a task ID, poll later\n- **Streaming:** Get SSE updates as the task progresses\n- **Push notifications:** A2A server sends callbacks when tasks complete\n\n#### Webhook Integration\n\nA2A push notifications enable webhook-style integration:\n\n```typescript\n// Configure push notification callback\nconst result = await client.sendMessage({\n  agentUrl: \"https://agent.example.com/a2a\",\n  message: { role: \"user\", parts: [{ type: \"text\", text: \"Long-running analysis\" }] },\n  pushNotification: {\n    url: \"https://your-server.example.com/webhook/a2a\",\n    authentication: { scheme: \"bearer\", credential: \"token\" }\n  }\n});\n```\n\n### Integration Testing Patterns\n\n#### Testing A2A Client Against a Real Server\n\n```typescript\n// tests/integration/a2a-client-integration.test.ts\nimport { describe, it, expect, beforeAll, afterAll } from \"vitest\";\nimport { A2AServer } from \"../../src/a2a-server.js\";\nimport { A2AClient } from \"../../src/a2a-client.js\";\n\ndescribe(\"A2A Client-Server Integration\", () => {\n  let server: A2AServer;\n  let baseUrl: string;\n\n  beforeAll(async () => {\n    server = new A2AServer({ port: 0, host: \"127.0.0.1\" });\n    await server.start();\n    baseUrl = `http://127.0.0.1:${server.port}`;\n  });\n\n  afterAll(async () => {\n    await server.stop();\n  });\n\n  it(\"should discover agent card\", async () => {\n    const discovery = new AgentDiscovery({ cacheEnabled: false });\n    const card = await discovery.discoverAgent(baseUrl);\n    expect(card).toBeDefined();\n    expect(card.name).toBe(\"pi-a2a-agent\");\n  });\n\n  it(\"should send message and receive response\", async () => {\n    const client = new A2AClient(clientConfig, securityConfig);\n    const result = await client.sendMessage({\n      agentUrl: baseUrl,\n      message: { role: \"user\", parts: [{ type: \"text\", text: \"Hello\" }] },\n    });\n    expect(result.status).toBe(\"completed\");\n  });\n});\n```\n\n#### Testing Extension Registration\n\n```typescript\n// tests/unit/extension-registration.test.ts\nimport { describe, it, expect, vi } from \"vitest\";\nimport extension from \"../../src/index.js\";\n\ndescribe(\"Extension Registration\", () => {\n  it(\"should register all 10 commands\", () => {\n    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };\n    extension(mockPi as any);\n    expect(mockPi.registerCommand).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(10);\n  });\n\n  it(\"should register 2 tools\", () => {\n    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };\n    extension(mockPi as any);\n    expect(mockPi.registerTool).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(2);\n  });\n\n  it(\"should subscribe to session_start and session_shutdown\", () => {\n    const mockPi = { on: vi.fn(), registerTool: vi.fn(), registerCommand: vi.fn() };\n    extension(mockPi as any);\n    expect(mockPi.on).toHaveBeenCalledWith(\"session_start\", expect.any(Function));\n    expect(mockPi.on).toHaveBeenCalledWith(\"session_shutdown\", expect.any(Function));\n  });\n});\n```\n\n---\n\n## Release & Distribution Practices\n\n### Versioning Strategy\n\nThis project follows **Semantic Versioning (semver)** with a pre-release phase:\n\n```
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH[-PRERELEASE]

Examples:
0.1.0-alpha.1   ← Pre-alpha: incomplete features, breaking changes expected\n
0.2.0-alpha.1   ← Alpha: feature-complete for milestone, spec compliance in progress\n
0.2.0-beta.1     ← Beta: all features working, testing for edge cases\n
0.2.0            ← Stable: ready for production use\n
1.0.0            ← Major: A2A v1.0 spec fully compliant, API stable
```

**When to bump each level:**\n\n| Change | Bump | Example |\n|--------|------|--------|\n| A2A protocol method changed (breaking) | MAJOR | `1.0.0` → `2.0.0` |\n| New command/tool added (non-breaking) | MINOR | `0.2.0` → `0.3.0` |\n| Spec compliance fix, bug fix | PATCH | `0.2.1` → `0.2.2` |\n| Spec compliance work in progress | PRERELEASE | `0.2.0-alpha.1` → `0.2.0-alpha.2` |\n\n### Dual Manifest Management\n\nThis project maintains **two manifests** that must be kept in sync:\n\n| File | Purpose | Key Fields |\n|------|---------|------------|\n| `package.json` | npm package (build, dependencies, scripts) | `name`, `version`, `main`, `exports`, `scripts`, `devDependencies` |\n| `pi-package.json` | pi extension (commands, tools, compatibility) | `name`, `version`, `commands`, `tools`, `compatibility`, `category` |\n\n**Synchronization checklist on version bump:**\n\n1. Update `version` in both `package.json` and `pi-package.json`\n2. Verify `commands` and `tools` in `pi-package.json` match `index.ts` registrations\n3. Verify `compatibility.node` matches `engines.node` in `package.json`\n4. Update `changes` array in `pi-package.json` with release notes\n5. Run `npm run build` to verify both manifests produce valid output\n\n### Release Workflow\n\n#### For Pre-release (alpha/beta)\n\n```bash\n# 1. Ensure all tests pass\nnpx vitest run\nnpx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance\n\n# 2. Bump pre-release version\nnpm version prerelease --preid=alpha  # 0.2.0-alpha.1 → 0.2.0-alpha.2\n# OR manually:\nnpm version 0.2.0-alpha.2\n\n# 3. Update pi-package.json version to match\n# (manual edit or script)\n\n# 4. Build\nnpm run build\n\n# 5. Publish to npm with pre-release tag\nnpm publish --tag alpha\n\n# 6. Git tag and push\ngit tag v0.2.0-alpha.2\ngit push origin main --tags\n\n# 7. Users install with\ncd ~ && pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@alpha\n```\n\n#### For Stable Release\n\n```bash\n# 1. Ensure all tests pass (including conformance)\nnpx vitest run\nnpx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance\n\n# 2. Update CHANGELOG (or let automation handle it)\n# See \"Changelog\" section below\n\n# 3. Bump version\nnpm version patch  # 0.2.0 → 0.2.1 (bug fix)\nnpm version minor  # 0.2.0 → 0.3.0 (new feature)\nnpm version major  # 0.x.0 → 1.0.0 (breaking change)\n\n# 4. Update pi-package.json version to match\n\n# 5. Build and verify\nnpm run build\nnpm publish --dry-run\n\n# 6. Publish to npm\nnpm publish\n\n# 7. Git tag and push\ngit tag v0.2.1\ngit push origin main --tags\n\n# 8. Users install with\npi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@0.2.1\n# OR\npi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.1\n```\n\n### Distribution Channels\n\nThis project supports **three distribution channels**:\n\n| Channel | Install Command | Use Case |\n|---------|----------------|----------|\n| **npm** | `pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@0.2.0` | Production, version-pinned installs |\n| **Git** | `pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0` | Development, custom branches, unreleased features |\n| **Local** | `pi install ./path/to/pi-a2a-communication` | Local development, testing unreleased changes |\n\n#### npm Distribution\n\n```jsonc\n// package.json — fields that control npm distribution\n{\n  \"name\": \"pi-a2a-communication\",\n  \"version\": \"0.2.0-alpha.1\",\n  \"main\": \"dist/index.js\",\n  \"types\": \"dist/index.d.ts\",\n  \"files\": [\n    \"dist/**/*\",\n    \"README.md\",\n    \"LICENSE\"\n  ],\n  \"keywords\": [\"pi\", \"pi-extension\", \"pi-package\", \"a2a\", \"agent2agent\", ...],\n  \"repository\": \"https://github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication\"\n}\n```\n\n**Key points:**\n- `files` field controls what's included in the npm tarball — only `dist/`, `README.md`, and `LICENSE`\n- Source maps and declarations are included for debugging\n- `keywords` must include `pi-package` for gallery discoverability\n\n#### Git Distribution\n\nPi handles git packages by:\n1. Cloning the repo to `~/.pi/agent/git/github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication/`\n2. Checking out the pinned ref (tag, branch, or commit)\n3. Running `npm install` (production only) if `package.json` exists\n4. Loading the extension from the `pi.extensions` or `pi.manifest` field\n\n**Best practices for git distribution:**\n- Always pin to a version tag: `pi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0`\n- Never rely on `main` branch alone — branches can have breaking changes\n- Ensure `npm install --omit=dev` succeeds in CI — all runtime deps must be in `dependencies`\n\n#### Local Development Distribution\n\n```bash\n# Link for active development (changes reflect immediately)\ncd ~/projects/pi-a2a-communication\nnpm link\n\n# Or add to settings.json manually\n# ~/.pi/agent/settings.json\n{\n  \"extensions\": [\n    \"/absolute/path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts\"\n  ]\n}\n\n# Or use -e for quick testing\npi -e /path/to/pi-a2a-communication/src/index.ts\n```\n\n**Note:** When using `npm link`, changes to `.ts` files require pi `/reload` to take effect. pi loads extensions via jiti, which compiles on-the-fly but caches.\n\n### Changelog Management\n\nMaintain a `CHANGELOG.md` following [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/) format:\n\n```markdown\n# Changelog\n\n## [0.2.0-alpha.2] - 2026-06-20\n\n### Added\n- SendStreamingMessage SSE support (S4 compliance)\n- Push notification client support (S5 compliance)\n- Task cancellation via `tasks/cancel` (S3b compliance)\n\n### Fixed\n- Agent Card path corrected to `/.well-known/agent-card` (S1)\n- JSON-RPC method names aligned with v1.0 spec (S2)\n\n### Changed\n- `a2a-server.ts` is now the primary target for M6 spec fixes\n- `executePiTask()` documented as stub (CA-3)\n\n## [0.1.0-alpha.1] - 2026-06-15\n\n### Added\n- Initial fork from DrOlu/pi-a2a-communication v1.0.1\n- A2A client, server, discovery, task manager\n- 10 slash commands, 2 tools\n- Vitest test suite with conformance tests\n```\n\n### Automated Release (GitHub Actions)\n\nRecommended release automation:\n\n```yaml\n# .github/workflows/release.yml\nname: Release\n\non:\n  push:\n    tags:\n      - \"v*\"\n\njobs:\n  release:\n    runs-on: ubuntu-latest\n    steps:\n      - uses: actions/checkout@v4\n      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4\n        with:\n          node-version: 20\n          cache: npm\n          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org\n\n      - run: npm ci\n      - run: npm run build\n      - run: npx vitest run\n      - run: npx vitest run a2a-v1-conformance\n\n      # Publish to npm\n      - run: npm publish --provenance\n        env:\n          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}\n\n      # Create GitHub release\n      - uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2\n        with:\n          generate_release_notes: true\n```\n\nThis triggers automatically when you push a version tag:\n\n```bash\nnpm version minor  # Creates commit + tag v0.3.0\ngit push origin main --tags  # Triggers release workflow\n```\n\n### Gallery Visibility\n\nTo appear in the [pi package gallery](https://pi.dev/packages), ensure:\n\n1. `package.json` includes `\"keywords\": [\"pi-package\", ...]`\n2. `pi-package.json` includes `category`, `tags`, and `compatibility`\n3. The package is published to npm with public visibility\n4. Optional: Add `video` or `image` fields to `pi-package.json` for gallery preview:\n\n```jsonc\n// pi-package.json\n{\n  \"video\": \"https://example.com/demo.mp4\",  // Autoplays on hover\n  \"image\": \"https://example.com/screenshot.png\"  // Static preview\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n## Common Deployment Scenarios\n\n### Scenario 1: Individual Developer (Local Agent)\n\nThe simplest deployment — one developer, one machine, calling remote A2A agents.\n\n```bash\npi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication\n```\n\n**What happens:**\n1. Pi clones the repo to `~/.pi/agent/git/github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication/`\n2. Runs `npm install --omit=dev`\n3. Loads `src/index.ts` via jiti (no build step needed for development)\n4. Extension registers 10 commands and 2 tools\n5. On session start, A2A client and discovery modules initialize\n\n**Use case:** Individual developer using `/a2a-send` to delegate tasks to remote agents (e.g., a research agent, a code review agent).\n\n### Scenario 2: Fleet Deployment (Multi-Node)\n\nDeploy across multiple machines running pi agents, each exposing an A2A server.\n\n```bash\n# On each fleet node:\npi install git:github.com/carlosfrias/pi-a2a-communication@v0.2.0\n\n# Configure (persists to ~/.pi/agent/a2a/config.json)\n/a2a-config server.enabled true\n/a2a-config server.port 10000\n/a2a-config server.host 0.0.0.0\n/a2a-config security.defaultScheme bearer\n/a2a-config security.bearerToken \"$A2A_BEARER_TOKEN\"\n\n# Start the A2A server\n/a2a-server start 10000\n```\n\n**Use case:** A fleet of 7 specialized pi agents (research, code review, deployment, monitoring, etc.) communicating via A2A protocol. Each agent discovers others via Agent Cards.\n\n### Scenario 3: A2A Gateway (Enterprise)\n\nFor production deployments with TLS, auth, and rate limiting, use **pi-a2a-gateway** (a separate project). This package is the **client** that connects to the gateway.\n\n```\n┌─────────────────┐      A2A Protocol      ┌──────────────────┐\n│  pi Agent       │ ◄──────────────────► │  A2A Gateway     │\n│  (this package) │    JSON-RPC over HTTP  │  (pi-a2a-gateway)│\n└─────────────────┘                        └────────┬─────────┘\n                                                    │\n                                    ┌───────────────┼───────────────┐\n                                    │               │               │\n                                    ▼               ▼               ▼\n                            ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐\n                            │ Auth Agent   │ │ Billing Agent│ │ ML Agent     │\n                            └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘\n```\n\n**Gateway provides:** TLS termination, OAuth2/mTLS authentication, rate limiting, agent registry, health monitoring, task queuing.\n\n**Use case:** Enterprise multi-organization agent collaboration with centralized management and security.\n\n### Scenario 4: CI/CD Pipeline Integration\n\nEmbed A2A tools in automated CI/CD pipelines via the pi SDK:\n\n```typescript\nimport {\n  createAgentSession,\n  DefaultResourceLoader,\n  SessionManager,\n} from \"@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent\";\n\nconst loader = new DefaultResourceLoader({\n  additionalExtensionPaths: [\"/opt/pi-extensions/pi-a2a-communication\"],\n});\nawait loader.reload();\n\nconst { session } = await createAgentSession({\n  resourceLoader: loader,\n  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),\n});\n\n// CI/CD pipeline: security scan → code review → deploy\nawait session.prompt(`\n  Use a2a_parallel to run security scans on these agents:\n  - https://security-agent.ci.internal\n  - https://compliance-agent.ci.internal\n  Then use a2a_call to request code review from https://review-agent.ci.internal\n`);\n```\n\n**Use case:** Automated pipelines where pi orchestrates multiple specialized agents for code review, security scanning, compliance checking, and deployment.\n\n### Scenario 5: A2A + MCP Combined Architecture\n\nAn agent uses **MCP for local tools** and **A2A for remote agents**:\n\n```\n┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n│                pi Agent                        │\n│                                                │\n│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────────┐  │\n│  │  MCP Tools   │    │  A2A Extension        │  │\n│  │  (local)     │    │  (remote agents)     │  │\n│  │              │    │                      │  │\n│  │ - Database   │    │ - Research Agent     │  │\n│  │ - File System│    │ - Code Review Agent  │  │\n│  │ - Git        │    │ - Deployment Agent   │  │\n│  │ - Browser    │    │ - Analytics Agent    │  │\n│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────────────┘  │\n└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n**Use case:** A pi agent that uses MCP to access databases and file systems locally, while delegating complex tasks (research, code review, analysis) to remote A2A agents. The agent combines both to produce comprehensive results.\n\n### Scenario 6: Multi-Tenant SaaS\n\nAn A2A gateway serving multiple organizations, each with their own agents:\n\n```\n┌──────────────────────────┐\n│    Organization A        │    ┌───────────────────┐\n│  ┌────────────────────┐  │    │  A2A Gateway      │\n│  │  pi Agent A1       │──┼───►│  (pi-a2a-gateway)  │\n│  │  (research)        │  │    │  - Auth (OAuth2)   │\n│  └────────────────────┘  │    │  - Rate Limiting   │\n│  ┌────────────────────┐  │    │  - Agent Registry  │\n│  │  pi Agent A2       │──┼───►│  - Health Monitor  │\n│  │  (code review)     │  │    └───────────────────┘\n│  └────────────────────┘  │            │\n└──────────────────────────┘            │\n │\n└──────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n**Configuration per tenant:**\n\n```jsonc\n// ~/.pi/agent/a2a/config.json (Organization A)\n{\n  \"client\": {\n    \"timeout\": 30000,\n    \"retryAttempts\": \"clientId\": \"org-a-pi-agent\",\n      \"clientSecret\": \"$ORG_A_CLIENT_SECRET\"\n    }\n  }\n}\n```\n\n**Use case:** SaaS platform where each organization has its own pi agents communicating sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),\n});\n\n// Programmatic A2A usage\nawait session.prompt(\"Use a2a_call to check the security agent\");\n```\n\n**Use case:** Building a custom web UI, CLI, 2. **Best practices** - Both general Node.js and pi-specific
3. **Development practices** - TDD, TypeScript, dual builds
4. **CI/CD practices** - GitHub Actions, npm publishing, automation

content.text:
You've got the reference docs. Now you're asking **where to build deep expertise** — the learning path, not just the reference. Let me give you a concrete roadmap.

## 🎯 Expertise Development Roadmap

### Phase 1: Foundation (Weeks 1-2)
**Goal: Understand the pi extension system from the inside out.**

| Step | Action | Resource |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Read the pi extensions docs end-to-end | `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/extensions.md` (you have this locally — I showed you the full doc) |
| 2 | Read the pi SDK docs end-to-end | `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/sdk.md` |
| 3 | Read the pi packages docs | `/usr/local/lib/node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/docs/packages.md` |
| 4 | Build a minimal extension from scratch | Create `~/.pi/agent/extensions/hello-world/index.ts` — a 20-line extension that registers one command and one tool |
| 5 | Study 2-3 existing extension examples | `pi-coding-agent/examples/extensions/` — read `dynamic-tools.ts` and `send-user-message.ts` |

**Hands-on exercise:** Write a pi extension from zero that:
- Registers a `/hello` command
- Registers a `greet` tool
- Subscribes to `session_start` and `tool_call` events
- Uses `ctx.ui.notify()` for user feedback

### Phase 2: Architecture Patterns (Weeks 3-4)
**Goal: Internalize plugin architecture patterns.**

| Step | Action | Resource |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Study this project's extension pattern | Read `src/index.ts` — trace how commands/tools/events are registered |
| 2 | Study the type stub pattern | Read `types/pi-runtime.d.ts` + `tsconfig.json` paths — understand host decoupling |
| 3 | Study the global state pattern | Trace the module-level `let` variables in `index.ts` — understand session lifecycle |
| 4 | Read about Node.js plugin architectures | [adaltas.com/en/2020/08/28/node-js-plugin-architecture](https://www.adaltas.com/en/2020/08/28/node-js-plugin-architecture/) — covers hook, middleware, and event-driven patterns |
| 5 | Compare approaches | Read how VS Code, Express, and Gatsby do extensions — then compare to pi's approach |

**Hands-on exercise:** Refactor a module in this project. Pick a small one like `config.ts` and add a new config option with a test first (TDD per CA-5).

### Phase 3: Testing & TDD (Weeks 5-6)
**Goal: Master testing a pi extension with type stubs and mocks.**

| Step | Action | Resource |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Read the conformance test suite | `tests/a2a-v1-conformance.test.ts` — understand how A2A protocol behavior is tested |
| 2 | Read the spec-compliance tests | `tests/spec-compliance/` — unit-level spec tests |
| 3 | Write a new test following the TDD pattern | Write a failing test for a new feature, then implement |
| 4 | Study Vitest mocking patterns | [vitest.dev/guide/mocking](https://vitest.dev/guide/mocking.html) — module mocking, `vi.fn()`, `vi.spyOn()` |
| 5 | Practice: mock ExtensionAPI | Create mock objects for `ExtensionAPI` and `ExtensionContext`, write tests that verify registration calls |

**Hands-on exercise:** Write a test that:
1. Creates a mock `ExtensionAPI`
2. Calls the extension factory
3. Asserts 10 commands and 2 tools are registered
4. Simulates `session_start` and verifies A2A client initialization
5. Simulates `session_shutdown` and verifies cleanup

### Phase 4: Build, Release & Distribution (Weeks 7-8)
**Goal: Ship a version and understand the full pipeline.**

| Step | Action | Resource |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Do a dry-run publish | `npm publish --dry-run` — understand what goes in the tarball |
| 2 | Set up GitHub Actions CI | Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with test + lint + build |
| 3 | Study npm trusted publishing | [docs.npmjs.com/trusted-publishers](https://docs.npmjs.com/trusted-publishers/) |
| 4 | Study semantic-release | [github.com/semantic-release/npm](https://github.com/semantic-release/npm) — automated versioning |
| 5 | Practice the full release workflow | Bump version → build → test → tag → publish → verify install |

**Hands-on exercise:** Set up a complete CI/CD pipeline:
1. Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (test on Node 18/20/22)
2. Create `.github/workflows/release.yml` (publish on tag)
3. Do a pre-release: `npm version prerelease --preid=alpha` → publish with `--tag alpha`
4. Verify install: `pi install npm:pi-a2a-communication@alpha`

### Phase 5: Integration & Real-World Deployment (Weeks 9-10)
**Goal: Deploy in a real multi-agent scenario.**

| Step | Action | Resource |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Set up a local A2A server | `/a2a-server start 10000` — discover it, send messages to it |
| 2 | Connect two pi agents via A2A | Run two pi sessions, each with the extension, call each other |
| 3 | Study the A2A v1.0 spec | [a2a-protocol.org/v1.0.0/specification](https://a2a-protocol.org/v1.0.0/specification/) |
| 4 | Complete the Google Codelab | [codelabs.developers.google.com/intro-a2a-purchasing-concierge](https://codelabs.developers.google.com/intro-a2a-purchasing-concierge) |
| 5 | Study the AWS A2A Gateway sample | [github.com/aws-samples/sample-a2a-gateway](https://github.com/aws-samples/sample-a2a-gateway) — 3-layer architecture pattern |
| 6 | Try the pi SDK embedding pattern | Write a standalone Node.js script that creates an agent session with A2A tools |

**Hands-on exercise:** Build a mini multi-agent system:
1. pi Agent A: research agent (exposes via `/a2a-server`)
2. pi Agent B: uses `/a2a-send` to delegate research tasks to Agent A
3. Verify the full round-trip: discovery → send → streaming response → completion

| semantic-release | [github.com/semantic-release/npm](https://github.com/semantic-release/npm) | Fully automated versioning + changelog + publish |
| Keep a Changelog | [keepachangelog.com](https://keepachangelog.com/) | The changelog standard |

### A2A Protocol (Deep Expertise)
| What | Where | Why |
|------|-------|-----|
| A2A v1.0 Spec (authoritative) | [a2a-protocol.org/v1.0.0/specification](https://a2a-protocol.org/v1.0.0/specification/) | The single source of truth — read this end-to-end |
| Google Codelab (hands-on) | [codelabs.developers.google.com/intro-a2a-purchasing-concierge](https://codelabs.developers.google.com/intro-a2a-purchasing-concierge) | Build a working multi-agent system step by step |
| AWS A2A Gateway (enterprise) | [github.com/aws-samples/sample-a2a-gateway](https://github.com/aws-samples/sample-a2a-gateway) | 3-layer architecture (management/control/data) |
| A2A vs MCP vs ACP | [tyk.io/learning-center/agent-protocols](https://tyk.io/learning-center/agent-protocols-a-complete-guide-to-mcp-a2a-and-acp/) | When to use which protocol |
| Enterprise A2A patterns | [xenoss.io/blog/agent2agent-a2a-protocol-enterprise-guide](https://xenoss.io/blog/agent2agent-a2a-protocol-enterprise-guide) | Security, scaling, multi-tenant |

## 🛠️ Recommended Learning Order

```
Week 1-2: pi Extension Basics
  ├── Read extensions.md, sdk.md, packages.md (local docs)
  ├── Build hello-world extension
  └── Study dynamic-tools.ts and send-user-message.ts examples

Week 3-4: Architecture Patterns
  ├── Read this project's index.ts (extension registration)
  ├── Study type stubs and global state patterns
  ├── Read adaltas plugin architecture article
  └── Compare VS Code vs Express vs pi extension models

Week 5-6: Testing & TDD
  ├── Read conformance tests (source of truth)
  ├── Write mock ExtensionAPI tests
  ├── Practice TDD: test → implement → refactor
  └── Study Vitest mocking

Week 7-8: Build & Release
  ├── Set up GitHub Actions CI
  ├── Dry-run publish
  ├── Study npm trusted publishing
  └── Complete a pre-release cycle

Week 9-10: Integration & Deployment
  ├── Run local A2A server + client round-trip
  ├── Complete Google Codelab
  ├── Study AWS Gateway architecture
  └── Build a 2-agent system
```

**The single most impactful step:** Read the local pi docs (`extensions.md`, `sdk.md`, `packages.md`) end-to-end, then build the hello-world extension. Everything else builds on that foundation.

## 🎓 Online Courses & Interactive Learning

## 📚 Books Worth Reading

| **"Node.js Design Patterns"** (3rd Ed) by Mario Casciarola | Chapter 5: Design Patterns (Observer, Strategy, Proxy), Chapter 10: Messaging patterns, Chapter 14: Module patterns | Plugin architecture, ESM/CJS, event systems |
| **"Secure by Design"** by Dan Bergh Johnsson et al. | Domain primitives, reducing attack surface — directly relevant to zero-dependency library design | Security for npm packages |

| **"Refactoring"** (2nd Ed) by Martin Fowler | The catalog of refactoring patterns — essential for the TDD cycle this project requires | Test → Refactor cycle |
